images.caption: Figure 1:Comparison of GUI grounding paradigms. (a) Direct grounding methods struggle with dense interfaces. (b) Iterative cropping methods incur large resource costs and use rigid cropping ratios. (c) Our UI-Zoomer applies Test-Time Scaling (TTS) with reliability gating, adaptively choosing between consensus voting and adaptive cropping, achieving stronger robustness with one-take time costs.

Figure 2:Overview of UI-Zoomer. (a) The model samplesNNcandidate predictions via Test-Time Scaling (TTS). (b) A reliability gate routes confident instances to consensus voting (Choice I) and uncertain ones to adaptive cropping (Choice II). (d) The crop window is derived from 2D Gaussian variance decomposition, enabling per-instance adaptive zoom-in.

Figure 3:Ablation on sampling temperatureTT(left) and number of candidatesNN(right)
on ScreenSpot-Pro.

Figure 4:Ablation study of the gating thresholdτ\tauand Gaussian spreadσ\sigmaon ScreenSpot-v2. Grey bars indicate the proportion of samples routed to the zoom-in cropping stage (CROP%), while blue curves show overall accuracy.

Figure 5:Histogram distributions ofCspatialC_{\mathrm{spatial}}anda​v​g​_​c​o​n​favg\_confon ScreenSpot-Pro (N=1581N=1581). The two signals exhibit complementary distributional characteristics, enabling a more discriminative gating mechanism when combined.

Figure 6:Here are four representative cases: the top two are successful examples, while the remaining two illustrate failure cases.
Blue boxes denote the eight sampled bounding boxes, red indicates the cropped region, green represents the ground-truth box, and yellow marks the final prediction obtained after zooming on the cropped image.
Figure 7:Complete prompt template used in our experiments.
